watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water 2-3 times a week, avoid wetting leaves)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Talong.description: Eggplant (in North American, Australian, and Philippine English), aubergine (in British, Irish, and New Zealand English), brinjal (in Indian, Bangladeshi, Pakistani, Singapore, Malaysian, South African, and Sri Lankan English), or baigan (in Caribbean English) is a plant species in the nightshade family Solanaceae. Solanum melongena is grown worldwide for its edible fruit, typically used as a vegetable in cooking. Most commonly purple, the spongy, absorbent fruit is used in several cuisines. It is a berry by botanical definition. As a member of the genus Solanum, it is related to the tomato, chili pepper, and potato, although those are of the Americas region while the eggplant is of the Eurasia region. Like the tomato, its skin and seeds can be eaten, but it is usually eaten cooked. Eggplant is nutritionally low in macronutrient and micronutrient content, but the capability of the fruit to absorb oils and flavors into its flesh through cooking expands its use in the culinary arts.
Talong.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Talong.uses: Used extensively in staple Filipino dishes, most famously grilled and beaten with eggs to make Tortang Talong, or added to stews like Pinakbet and Kare-Kare.

Talong___Healthy.symptoms: Green leaves, strong stems, normal blossoms, and shiny dark purple fruits.
Talong___Healthy.organic_treatment: No treatment needed. Keep maintaining watering schedule.
Talong___Healthy.preventive_measures: Weed regularly and check underneath leaves for early insect arrivals.

Talong___Bacterial_Wilt.symptoms: Rapid wilting starting from the top, even when soil is wet. Stem interior turns brown.
Talong___Bacterial_Wilt.organic_treatment: No organic cure exists. Immediately uproot and burn/bury the plant to prevent soil contamination.
Talong___Bacterial_Wilt.preventive_measures: Practice strict crop rotation. Improve soil drainage.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water daily during dry periods, maintain drainage)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Ampalaya.description: Momordica charantia (commonly called bitter melon, gouya, cerassee, goya, bitter apple, bitter gourd, bitter squash, balsam-pear, karela, karavila and many more names listed below) is a tropical and subtropical vine of the family Cucurbitaceae, widely grown in Asia, Africa, and the Caribbean for its edible fruit. Its many varieties differ substantially in the shape and bitterness of the fruit. Bitter melon originated in Africa, where it was a dry-season staple food of ǃKung hunter-gatherers. Wild or semi-domesticated variants spread across Asia in prehistory, and it was likely fully domesticated in Southeast Asia. It is widely used in the cuisines of East Asia, South Asia, and Southeast Asia.
Ampalaya.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Ampalaya.uses: Widely harvested for everyday savory home cooking, serving as a core ingredient in traditional dishes like Pinakbet or famously sautéed with eggs (Gisang Ampalaya).

Ampalaya___Healthy.symptoms: Vigorous crawling vines, bright yellow flowers, and textured green bitter melons.
Ampalaya___Healthy.organic_treatment: None. Ensure trellis support is sturdy.
Ampalaya___Healthy.preventive_measures: Consistent watering helps reduce extreme bitterness in the harvest.

Ampalaya___Downy_Mildew.symptoms: Yellow angular spots on top of leaves; gray/purplish mold layer underneath leaves.
Ampalaya___Downy_Mildew.organic_treatment: Prune infected leaves to open up airflow. Spray with baking soda or wood ash solution.
Ampalaya___Downy_Mildew.preventive_measures: Avoid planting cucurbits too close to one another.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Keep soil evenly moist, avoid waterlogging)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Kamatis.description: The tomato (US: /təˈmeɪtoʊ/, UK: /təˈmɑːtoʊ/; Solanum lycopersicum) is a plant whose fruit is an edible berry that is eaten as a vegetable. The tomato is a member of the nightshade family that includes tobacco, potato, and chili peppers. It originated from western South America, and may have been domesticated there, in Mexico, or in Central America. The Spanish introduced tomatoes to Eurasia in the Columbian exchange in the 16th century. Tomato plants are vines, largely annual, and vulnerable to frost, though sometimes living longer in greenhouses. The flowers can self-fertilize. Modern varieties have been bred to ripen uniformly red, in a process that has impaired the fruit's sweetness and flavor. There are thousands of cultivars, varying in size, color, shape, and flavor. Tomatoes are attacked by many insect pests and nematodes and are subject to diseases caused by viruses, mildew, and blight fungi.
Kamatis.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Kamatis.uses: A crucial souring and savory base sautéed in everyday dishes (Gisa), served raw as a side salad with salted egg (Itlog na Maalat), or added to Sinigang.

Kamatis___Healthy.symptoms: Robust green leaves, sturdy hair-covered stems, yellow flowers, and firm round fruits.
Kamatis___Healthy.preventive_measures: Mulch the soil surface to keep rain from splashing soil-borne fungi onto foliage.

Kamatis___Late_Blight.symptoms: Dark brown patches on leaves; fuzzy white mold growth under leaves in humid mornings.
Kamatis___Late_Blight.organic_treatment: Remove infected foliage. Spray with copper sulfate solution or baking soda mixture.
Kamatis___Late_Blight.preventive_measures: Water at the roots; keep leaves dry.

Kamatis___Yellow_Leaf_Curl.condition_name: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (Kulot na Dahon)
Kamatis___Yellow_Leaf_Curl.symptoms: Upward curling and yellowing leaf edges; stunted plant; no fruit setting.
Kamatis___Yellow_Leaf_Curl.organic_treatment: Control whiteflies using neem oil or soap spray. Uproot infected plants to prevent spread.
Kamatis___Yellow_Leaf_Curl.preventive_measures: Plant marigolds nearby to repel insect vectors.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water when topsoil is dry, drought-tolerant once established)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Sili.description: Capsicum frutescens is a wild chili pepper that has genetic proximity to the cultivated pepper Capsicum chinense. It is native to Central and South America as well as Mexico. Pepper cultivars of C. frutescens can be annual or short-lived perennial plants. The flowers of Capsicum frutescens are white, with a greenish white or greenish yellow corolla. They are either insect or self-pollinated.
Sili.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Sili.uses: Harvested as an indispensable culinary spice, aromatic, or hot condiment to add signature heat and flavor enhancement to local dipping sauces, stews, and sizzling dishes.

Sili___Healthy.symptoms: Lush green foliage, small white flowers, and firm upright or hanging peppers.
Sili___Healthy.preventive_measures: Provide full sun and avoid waterlogging.

Sili___Anthracnose.symptoms: Sunken circular dark spots on fruits; fruits rot and dry up on the branch.
Sili___Anthracnose.organic_treatment: Remove and burn affected fruits immediately. Spray with garlic/neem solution.
Sili___Anthracnose.preventive_measures: Only save seeds from completely healthy fruits.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Requires steady watering at the base, 1-2 inches per week)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Kalabasa.description: Cucurbita moschata is a species originating in the tropical Americas which is cultivated for edible flesh, flowers, greens, and seeds. It includes cultivars known in English as squash or pumpkin. Cultivars of C. moschata are generally more tolerant of hot, humid weather than squash of other domesticated species. C. moschata also exhibit a greater resistance to certain disease and insects, notably including to the squash vine borer. Commercially-made pumpkin pie mix is most often made from varieties of C. moschata. All species of squashes and pumpkins are native to the Western Hemisphere, and the ancestral members of the genus Cucurbita were present in the Americas before humans. Squash are important food plants of the original people of the region, ranking next to maize and beans in many precolonial American economies.
Kalabasa.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Kalabasa.uses: Highly valued for sweetening and thickening local stews, commonly paired with sitaw in Ginisang Kalabasa at Sitaw, or cooked in coconut milk (Ginataang Kalabasa).

Kalabasa___Healthy.symptoms: Large trailing vines, huge yellow flowers, and heavy firm squashes.
Kalabasa___Healthy.preventive_measures: Ensure soil is rich in organic compost.

Kalabasa___Powdery_Mildew.symptoms: White powder spots on leaves, leading to yellowing and premature leaf fall.
Kalabasa___Powdery_Mildew.organic_treatment: Spray with 1:9 milk-water mixture or baking soda spray. Expose to full sun.
Kalabasa___Powdery_Mildew.preventive_measures: Prune overlapping leaves to allow airflow.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Drought-tolerant, but waters every 2-3 days for high yield)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Okra.description: Okra (US: /ˈoʊkrə/, UK: /ˈɒkrə/), Abelmoschus esculentus, known in some English-speaking countries as lady's fingers, is a flowering plant in the mallow family native to East Africa. Cultivated in tropical, subtropical, and warm temperate regions around the world for its edible green (red in some cultivars) seed pods, okra is used in the cuisines of many countries. The species is a perennial, often cultivated as an annual in temperate climates, often growing to around 2 metres (6 ft 7 in) tall. As a member of the Malvaceae, it is related to such species as cotton, cocoa, and hibiscus. The leaves are 10–20 centimetres (4–8 in) long and broad, palmately lobed with 5–7 lobes. The flowers are 4–8 cm (1+5⁄8–3+1⁄8 in) in diameter, with five white to yellow petals, often with a red or purple spot at the base. The pollen grains are spherical and approximately 188 microns in diameter. The fruit is a capsule up to 18 cm (7 in) long with pentagonal cross-section, containing numerous seeds.
Okra.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Okra.uses: Provides a characteristic thick, mucilaginous texture to traditional boiled vegetable stews such as Pinakbet, Dinengdeng, and Sinigang.

Okra___Healthy.symptoms: Tall erect stem, cream flowers, and green pods pointing upwards.
Okra___Healthy.preventive_measures: Harvest pods every 2 days to encourage continuous fruiting.

Okra___Yellow_Vein_Mosaic.symptoms: Bright yellow leaf veins; stunted yellow leaves; tough, pale pods.
Okra___Yellow_Vein_Mosaic.organic_treatment: Control whitefly vectors using neem oil. Pull out infected plants.
Okra___Yellow_Vein_Mosaic.preventive_measures: Keep garden free of weeds that host insect pests.

watering_needs.frequency: High (Needs consistent soil moisture, water daily)
watering_needs.description: Requires consistent and abundant soil moisture. Highly susceptible to drying out; water daily and protect from extreme dry spells.

Pechay.description: Bok choy (American English, Canadian English, and Australian English), pak choi (British English, South African English, and Caribbean English) or pok choi is a type of Chinese cabbage (Brassica rapa subsp. chinensis) cultivated as a leaf vegetable to be used as food. Varieties do not form heads and have green leaf blades with lighter bulbous bottoms instead, forming a cluster reminiscent of mustard greens. Its flavor is described as being between spinach and water chestnuts but slightly sweeter, with a mildly peppery undertone. The green leaves have a stronger flavor than the white bulb. Chinensis varieties are popular in southern China, East Asia, and Southeast Asia. Originally classified as Brassica chinensis by Carl Linnaeus, they are now considered a subspecies of Brassica rapa. They are a member of the family Brassicaceae.
Pechay.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Pechay.uses: Its crisp stalks and tender green leaves are commonly added at the end of cooking in savory beef and pork broths like Nilaga, Bulalo, and Pochero.

Pechay___Healthy.symptoms: Crisp green leaves with broad white stems growing in a tight cluster.
Pechay___Healthy.preventive_measures: Use garden netting to protect leaves from caterpillars.

Pechay___Black_Rot.symptoms: V-shaped yellow leaf margins; black leaf veins; foul-smelling rot at the base.
Pechay___Black_Rot.organic_treatment: None. Remove and discard affected crops. Disinfect soil.
Pechay___Black_Rot.preventive_measures: Ensure excellent drainage; avoid planting in waterlogged soils.

watering_needs.frequency: High (Grows in water or muddy soil, keep saturated)
watering_needs.description: Requires consistent and abundant soil moisture. Highly susceptible to drying out; water daily and protect from extreme dry spells.

Kangkong.description: Ipomoea aquatica, commonly known as water spinach or kangkung, is a semi-aquatic, tropical plant grown as a vegetable for its tender shoots. I. aquatica is generally believed to have been first domesticated in Southeast Asia. It is widely cultivated in Southeast Asia, East Asia, and South Asia. It grows abundantly near waterways and requires little to no care. Ipomoea aquatica grows in water or on moist soil. Its stems are 2–3 metres (7–10 ft) or longer, rooting at the nodes. The hollow cavity within the stem makes the plant buoyant. The leaves vary from typically sagittate (arrowhead-shaped) to lanceolate, 5–15 cm (2–6 in) long and 2–8 cm (0.8–3 in) broad. The flowers are trumpet-shaped, 3–5 cm (1–2 in) in diameter, and usually white in colour with a mauve centre. Propagation is either by planting cuttings of the stem shoots, which will root along nodes, or by planting the seeds from flowers that produce seed pods.
Kangkong.habitat_ecology: Thrives in marshes, flooded muddy banks, waterways, and wetland gardens across the Philippine islands.
Kangkong.uses: A versatile leafy green frequently sautéed with garlic and soy sauce (Adobong Kangkong), fried crisp as an appetizer, or floated in sour Sinigang broth.

Kangkong___Healthy.symptoms: Hollow green trailing stems and narrow leaves growing aggressively.

Kangkong___White_Rust.symptoms: White chalky pustules underneath leaves; leaves curl, deform, and drop.
Kangkong___White_Rust.organic_treatment: Remove infected leaves. Spray with dilute baking soda solution.
Kangkong___White_Rust.preventive_measures: Keep planting area clear of debris; water roots directly.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water at base when soil feels dry)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Sitaw.description: The asparagus bean (Vigna unguiculata subsp. sesquipedalis) is a legume cultivated for its edible green pods containing immature seeds, like the green bean. It is also known as yardlong bean, pea bean, long-podded cowpea, Chinese long bean, snake bean, bodi, borboti, and bora. Despite the common name of "yardlong", the pods are actually only about half a yard long, so the subspecies name sesquipedalis (one-and-a-half-foot-long; 1.5 feet (0.50 yd)) is a more accurate approximation. A variety of the cowpea, the asparagus bean is grown primarily for its strikingly long (35 to 75 centimetres (1.15 to 2.46 ft)) immature green pods and has uses very similar to those of the green bean. This plant is in a different genus from the common bean. The different colors of seeds usually distinguish the many varieties. It is a vigorous climbing annual vine. The plant is subtropical/tropical and most widely grown in the warmer parts of South Asia, Southeast Asia, and southern China.
Sitaw.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Sitaw.uses: A classic Bahay Kubo long bean used in Pinakbet, Ginataang Kalabasa, or sautéed with soy sauce and pork in Adobong Sitaw.

Sitaw___Healthy.symptoms: Climbing green vines, purple flowers, and long slender green hanging bean pods.
Sitaw___Healthy.preventive_measures: Regularly monitor shoot tips for aphid infestations.

Sitaw___Rust.symptoms: Small reddish-brown powdery spots underneath leaves, causing early drying and shedding.
Sitaw___Rust.organic_treatment: Spray with sulfur dust or neem oil. Prune lower infested leaves.
Sitaw___Rust.preventive_measures: Avoid working around plants when they are wet.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Drought-resistant, water weekly during dry spells)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Kamote.description: The sweet potato or sweetpotato (Ipomoea batatas) is a dicotyledonous plant in the morning glory family, Convolvulaceae. Its sizeable, starchy, sweet-tasting tuberous roots are used as a root vegetable, which is a staple food in parts of the world. Cultivars of the sweet potato have been bred to bear tubers with flesh and skin of various colors. Moreover, the young shoots and leaves are occasionally eaten as greens. The sweet potato and the potato are only distantly related, both being in the order Solanales. Although darker sweet potatoes are often known as yams in parts of North America, they are even more distant from actual yams, which are monocots in the order Dioscoreales. The sweet potato is native to the tropical regions of South America in modern Ecuador. Of the approximately 50 genera and more than 1,000 species of Convolvulaceae, I. batatas is the only crop plant of major importance—some others are used locally (e.g., I. aquatica 'kangkong' as a green vegetable), but many are poisonous. The genus Ipomoea that contains the sweet potato also includes several garden flowers called morning glories, but that term is not usually extended to I. batatas. Some cultivars of I. batatas are grown as ornamental plants under the name tuberous morning glory and sometimes used in a horticultural context. Sweet potatoes can also be called yams in North America.
Kamote.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Kamote.uses: Its starchy tubers are boiled, fried, and caramelized into popular street snacks like Kamote Cue, while the young leaves (Talbos ng Kamote) are boiled for salads.

Kamote___Healthy.symptoms: Vibrant heart-shaped creeping vines; robust underground sweet potato tubers.
Kamote___Healthy.preventive_measures: Add soil around the base (hilling-up) to support tuber growth.

Kamote___Feathered_Mottle.symptoms: Faint purple feather-like patterns along leaf veins; smaller tuber yields.
Kamote___Feathered_Mottle.organic_treatment: Control aphids with soap spray. Prune symptomatic vines.

watering_needs.frequency: High (Likes swampy or highly damp soil)
watering_needs.description: Requires consistent and abundant soil moisture. Highly susceptible to drying out; water daily and protect from extreme dry spells.

Gabi.description: Taro (/ˈtɑːroʊ, ˈtær-/; Colocasia esculenta) is a root vegetable. It is the most widely cultivated species of several plants in the family Araceae that are used as vegetables for their corms, leaves, stems and petioles. Taro corms are a food staple in African, Caribbean, Oceanian, East Asian, Southeast Asian and South Asian cultures (similar to yams). Colocasia esculenta is a perennial, tropical plant primarily grown as a root vegetable for its edible, starchy corm. The plant has rhizomes of different shapes and sizes. Leaves are up to 40 by 25 centimetres (15+1⁄2 by 10 inches) and sprout from the rhizome. They are dark green above and light green beneath. They are triangular-ovate, sub-rounded and mucronate at the apex, with the tip of the basal lobes rounded or sub-rounded. The petiole is .8–1.2 metres (2+1⁄2–4 feet) high. The path can be up to 25 cm (10 in) long. The spadix is about three fifths as long as the spathe, with flowering parts up to 8 millimetres (3⁄8 in) in diameter. The female portion is at the fertile ovaries intermixed with sterile white ones. Neuters grow above the females, and are rhomboid or irregular orium lobed, with six or eight cells. The appendage is shorter than the male portion.
Gabi.habitat_ecology: Thrives in marshes, flooded muddy banks, waterways, and wetland gardens across the Philippine islands.
Gabi.uses: The starchy corm is simmered to thicken and flavor Sinigang broth, while the leaves and stalks are famously shredded and cooked in coconut milk to make Laing.

Gabi___Healthy.symptoms: Large elephant-ear leaves, upright green petioles, healthy underground corms.
Gabi___Healthy.preventive_measures: Ensure constant soil moisture but avoid stagnant pooling.

Gabi___Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Large purple-brown water-soaked spots on leaves; yellow fluid oozes from lesions.
Gabi___Leaf_Blight.organic_treatment: Prune infected leaves. Spray copper fungicide or baking soda solution.
Gabi___Leaf_Blight.preventive_measures: Use disease-free corms. Space out plants to reduce humidity.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Keep moist during early growth, dry out before harvest)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Ubi.description: Dioscorea alata – also called ube, ubi, uwhi, purple yam, or greater yam, among many other names – is a species of yam (a tuber). The tubers are usually a vivid violet-purple to bright lavender (hence the common name), but some range from creamy-white to plain white. Although it is sometimes confused with taro and the Okinawa sweet potato beniimo (紅芋) (Ipomoea batatas 'Ayamurasaki'), D. alata is also grown in Okinawa. Its origins are in the Asian and Oceanian tropics. Some varieties attain to great size. A "Mambatap" greater yam grown in Maprik, East Sepik District, Papua New Guinea around 1939 was 3.5 m (11 ft) in length. The name ube is derived from Tagalog in the Philippines while uwhi derives from Māori in New Zealand; both are doublets ultimately from Proto-Austronesian *qubi.
Ubi.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Ubi.uses: Almost exclusively boiled, mashed, and cooked with condensed milk and butter to make Halaya, serving as a signature flavoring for local pastries and ice cream.

Ubi___Healthy.symptoms: Climbing green heart-shaped vines, normal leaf growth, healthy purple tubers.
Ubi___Healthy.preventive_measures: Ensure strong trellising and loose soil for root expansion.

Ubi___Anthracnose.symptoms: Black spots on leaves and vines; vines die back rapidly, leading to small rotten tubers.
Ubi___Anthracnose.organic_treatment: Prune and destroy infected leaves. Spray copper fungicide or neem oil.
Ubi___Anthracnose.preventive_measures: Avoid overhead watering; clean trellis structures.

watering_needs.frequency: Low (Prefers dry, sandy soil; stop watering when leaves yellow)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Bawang.description: Garlic (Allium sativum) is a species of bulbous flowering plants in the genus Allium. Its close relatives include the onion, shallot, leek, chives, Welsh onion, and Chinese onion. Garlic is native to Central Asia, northeastern Iran and the foothills of Himalayas. It has naturalized in many other parts of the world, including Mediterranean Europe and China. There are two subspecies and hundreds of varieties of garlic. Garlic has been used for thousands of years as a seasoning, culinary ingredient, and traditional medical remedy. It was known in many ancient civilizations, including the Babylonians, Egyptians, Jews, Romans, and Chinese, and remains significant in many cuisines and folk treatments, especially across the Mediterranean and Asia. Sulfur compounds give garlic its pungent odor and flavor. There is insufficient clinical evidence to indicate that consuming garlic has any health benefits.
Bawang.habitat_ecology: Found across various open fields, agricultural plots, and forest areas in the Philippines.
Bawang.uses: An indispensable aromatic sauté base for almost all Filipino dishes, and famously fried golden-brown to top garlic fried rice (Sinangag).

Bawang___Healthy.symptoms: Erect blue-green leaves, healthy garlic bulb swelling underground.
Bawang___Healthy.preventive_measures: Limit watering as harvest time approaches to prevent bulb rot.

Bawang___Purple_Blotch.symptoms: Oval purple lesions on leaves; spots expand and dry out leaves, preventing bulb development.
Bawang___Purple_Blotch.organic_treatment: Prune dry leaves. Spray wood ash wash or organic copper spray.
Bawang___Purple_Blotch.preventive_measures: Ensure excellent soil drainage and avoid overwatering.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water weekly; stop watering 2 weeks before harvest)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Sibuyas.description: The onion (Allium cepa L., from Latin cepa), also known as the bulb onion or common onion, is a vegetable that is the most widely cultivated species of the genus Allium. The shallot is a botanical variety of the onion which was classified as a separate species until 2011. The onion's close relatives include garlic, scallion, leek, and chives. The genus contains several other species cultivated for food, such as the Japanese bunching onion Allium fistulosum, the tree onion Allium × proliferum, and the Canada onion Allium canadense. The name wild onion is applied to a number of Allium species, but A. cepa is exclusively known from cultivation. Its ancestral wild original form is not known, although escapes from cultivation have become established in some regions. The onion is most frequently a biennial or a perennial plant, but is usually treated as an annual and harvested in its first growing season.
Sibuyas.habitat_ecology: Found across various open fields, agricultural plots, and forest areas in the Philippines.
Sibuyas.uses: An indispensable aromatic base used in almost all savory Filipino dishes, stews, and sautéed meals.

Sibuyas___Healthy.symptoms: Hollow green upright tubular leaves, clean red or white onion bulbs.
Sibuyas___Healthy.preventive_measures: Mulch beds to maintain soil temperature and suppress weeds.

Sibuyas___Purple_Blotch.symptoms: Water-soaked spots on leaves that turn purple with yellow halos, causing leaves to collapse.
Sibuyas___Purple_Blotch.organic_treatment: Remove infected leaves. Spray with neem extract or copper fungicide.
Sibuyas___Purple_Blotch.preventive_measures: Practice crop rotation with non-allium crops.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Needs consistent moisture to keep tubers sweet)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Singkamas.description: Pachyrhizus erosus, commonly known as jícama (/ˈhɪkəmə/ or /dʒɪˈkɑːmə/; Spanish: jícama ; from Nahuatl languages: xīcamatl ) or Mexican turnip, is a native Mesoamerican vine, although the name jícama most commonly refers to the plant's edible tuberous root.It is in the pea family (Fabaceae). Pachyrhizus tuberosus and Pachyrhizus ahipa are the other two cultivated species in the genus. The naming of this group of edible plants can sometimes be confusing, with much overlap of similar, or the same, common names. Flowers, either blue or white, and pods similar to peas, are produced on fully developed plants. Several species of Pachyrhizus are known as jícama, but the one found in many markets is P. erosus. The two cultivated forms of P. erosus are jícama de agua and jícama de leche, both named for the consistency of their juice. The leche form has an elongated root and milky juice, while the agua form has a top-shaped to oblate root and a more watery, translucent juice and is the preferred form for the market.
Singkamas.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Singkamas.uses: Eaten fresh and raw as a crisp, refreshing snack with rock salt or vinegar, and widely used as a structural filling in fresh or fried Lumpia.

Singkamas___Healthy.symptoms: Creeping green vines, white/blue flowers, sweet crisp tubers underground.
Singkamas___Healthy.preventive_measures: Pinch off flower buds to redirect energy into tuber development.

Singkamas___Mosaic_Virus.symptoms: Mottled yellow-green mosaic patterns on leaves; deformed, curled leaves; small woody tubers.
Singkamas___Mosaic_Virus.organic_treatment: Spray soap solution to control aphid carriers. Uproot infected vines.
Singkamas___Mosaic_Virus.preventive_measures: Use certified seed sources and weed surrounding plots.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Keep soil evenly moist to avoid woody texture)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Labanos.description: The radish (Raphanus sativus or Raphanus raphanistrum subsp. sativus) is a flowering plant in the mustard family, Brassicaceae. Its large taproot is commonly used as a root vegetable, although the entire plant is edible and its leaves are sometimes used as a leaf vegetable. Originally domesticated in Asia, radishes are now grown and consumed globally. The radish is sometimes considered to form a species complex with the wild radish and instead treated as the subspecies Raphanus raphanistrum subsp. sativus. Radishes are often used raw as a crunchy salad vegetable with a pungent, slightly spicy flavor, varying in intensity depending on its growing environment. There are numerous varieties varying in size, flavor, color, and length of time they take to mature. Radishes owe their sharp flavor to the various chemical compounds produced by the plants, including glucosinolate, myrosinase, and isothiocyanate. They are sometimes grown as companion plants and suffer from few pests and diseases. They germinate quickly and grow rapidly, common smaller varieties being ready for consumption within a month, while larger daikon varieties take several weeks. Being relatively easy to grow and quick to harvest, radishes are often planted by novice gardeners. Another use of radish is as a cover or catch crop in winter, or as a forage crop. Some radishes are grown for their seeds; others, such as daikon, may be grown for oil production. Others are used for sprouting.
Labanos.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Labanos.uses: Harvested for its crisp, pungent white taproot, serving as an essential souring companion in savory dishes like Sinigang.

Labanos___Healthy.symptoms: Lush green lobed leaves radiating from a central white radish bulb.
Labanos___Healthy.preventive_measures: Keep soil moist to avoid spicy, woody, and split radishes.

Labanos___Clubroot.symptoms: Wilting during hot days; radish roots become swollen, deformed, and rot with a bad smell.
Labanos___Clubroot.organic_treatment: Uproot and discard. Apply wood ash/lime to the soil to raise pH above 7.2.
Labanos___Clubroot.preventive_measures: Avoid planting brassicas in the same spot for at least 3 years.

watering_needs.frequency: High (Water daily; water stress makes leaves very bitter)
watering_needs.description: Requires consistent and abundant soil moisture. Highly susceptible to drying out; water daily and protect from extreme dry spells.

Mustasa.description: Brassica juncea, commonly mustard greens, brown mustard, Chinese mustard, Indian mustard, Japanese mustard, Korean green mustard, leaf mustard, Oriental mustard and vegetable mustard, is a species of mustard plant. Brassica juncea cultivars can be divided into four major subgroups: integrifolia, juncea, napiformis, and tsatsai.
Mustasa.habitat_ecology: Thrives in the cool, high-altitude climates of the Philippines, heavily cultivated in agricultural regions like Benguet (Baguio) and high-elevation zones of Mindanao.
Mustasa.uses: Commonly fermented into Burong Mustasa, served as a tangy side dish for grilled fish, or cooked in fish broth (Sinigang sa Miso).

Mustasa___Healthy.symptoms: Broad, crinkled green leaves growing in a robust rosette.
Mustasa___Healthy.preventive_measures: Provide light shade during extremely hot afternoons.

Mustasa___DBM_Caterpillar.symptoms: Numerous small holes on leaves; leaves resemble lace; small green caterpillars visible under leaves.
Mustasa___DBM_Caterpillar.organic_treatment: Hand-pick caterpillars. Spray with homemade chili-garlic extract or soapy water.
Mustasa___DBM_Caterpillar.preventive_measures: Use insect-proof netting or plant companion onions/garlic.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water 2-3 times a week; requires excellent drainage)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Sayote.description: Chayote (/tʃaɪˈoʊteɪ/; previously placed in the obsolete genus Sechium, now in the genus Sicyos, as Sicyos edulis), also known as christophine, mirliton, güisquil, and choko, is an edible plant belonging to the gourd family, Cucurbitaceae. This fruit was first cultivated in Mesoamerica between southern Mexico and Honduras, with the most genetic diversity available in both Mexico and Guatemala. It is one among dozens of foods introduced to the Old World during the Columbian Exchange. At that time, the plant spread to other parts of the Americas, ultimately causing it to be integrated into the cuisine of many Latin American nations. When cooked, chayote is usually handled like summer squash; it is generally lightly cooked to retain the crispy consistency. Raw chayote may be added to salads or salsas, most often marinated with lemon or lime juice, or eaten raw. Whether raw or cooked, chayote is a good source of Vitamin C.
Sayote.habitat_ecology: Thrives in the cool, high-altitude climates of the Philippines, heavily cultivated in agricultural regions like Benguet (Baguio) and high-elevation zones of Mindanao.
Sayote.uses: Used as an affordable, mild-flavored filler in sautéed chicken dishes like Tinola, or sliced and stir-fried with garlic and pork.

Sayote___Healthy.symptoms: Vigorous vines crawling over overhead trellises, hanging pear-shaped green chayotes.
Sayote___Healthy.preventive_measures: Prune old vines after harvest to promote new shoots.

Sayote___Fruit_Fly.symptoms: Brown puncture marks on fruit; fruit oozes sap, turns yellow prematurely, and rots internally with small larvae.
Sayote___Fruit_Fly.organic_treatment: Wrap young fruit in paper bags. Hang yellow sticky traps nearby.
Sayote___Fruit_Fly.preventive_measures: Collect and bury fallen fruits at least 2 feet deep.

watering_needs.frequency: High (Water frequently to maintain tender leaf growth)
watering_needs.description: Requires consistent and abundant soil moisture. Highly susceptible to drying out; water daily and protect from extreme dry spells.

Alugbati.description: Basella alba is a perennial vine in the family Basellaceae. It is also known by common names including Malabar spinach, vine spinach, Ceylon spinach, alugbati and Indian spinach. The plant is native to Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, and New Guinea and naturalized elsewhere. It has a number of culinary uses.
Alugbati.habitat_ecology: Thrives in marshes, flooded muddy banks, waterways, and wetland gardens across the Philippine islands.
Alugbati.uses: Its thick, mucilage-rich leaves and purple stems are commonly added to soupy mung bean stews (Ginisang Monggo) or vegetable soup (Utan/Laswa).

Alugbati___Healthy.symptoms: Thick red or green fleshy stems, glossy heart-shaped leaves climbing vigorously.
Alugbati___Healthy.preventive_measures: Prune tips regularly to encourage lateral branching and tender growth.

Alugbati___Leaf_Spot.symptoms: Circular grayish spots with reddish-purple margins on leaves, causing them to dry up.
Alugbati___Leaf_Spot.organic_treatment: Prune infected leaves. Spray with dilute baking soda solution.
Alugbati___Leaf_Spot.preventive_measures: Avoid wetting foliage; improve air circulation.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water regularly, especially during fruit production)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Patola.description: Luffa acutangula is a cucurbitaceous vine commercially grown for its unripe fruits as a vegetable. Mature fruits are used as natural cleaning sponges. Its fruit slightly resembles a cucumber or zucchini with ridges. It is native to South Asia and has been naturalised in other regions. It is also grown as a houseplant in places with colder climates. English common English names include angled luffa, Chinese okra, dish cloth gourd, ridged gourd, sponge gourd, vegetable gourd, strainer vine, ribbed loofah, silky gourd, and silk gourd. The young fruits of some Luffa cultivars are used as cooked vegetables, pickled or eaten raw, and the shoots and flowers are sometimes also used. Like Luffa aegyptiaca, the mature fruits are harvested when dry and processed to remove all but the fruit fibre, which can then be used as a sponge or as fibre for making hats.
Patola.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Patola.uses: Its spongy flesh absorbs flavors beautifully, making it the primary vegetable in Misua soup cooked with meatballs and thin flour noodles.

Patola___Healthy.symptoms: Crawling green vines, yellow flowers, straight long gourds.
Patola___Healthy.preventive_measures: Allow fruits to hang freely from the trellis to prevent curved fruits.

Patola___Fruit_Fly.symptoms: Curved deformed gourds with entry wounds; yellow rot inside with tiny wiggling maggots.
Patola___Fruit_Fly.organic_treatment: Bag the patola fruits in newspaper bags right after flower drop. Use pheromone traps.
Patola___Fruit_Fly.preventive_measures: Dispose of all infected fruits away from the garden.

watering_needs.frequency: Low to Moderate (Very drought-tolerant once established)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Malunggay.description: Moringa oleifera is a short-lived, fast-growing, drought-resistant tree of the family Moringaceae, native to northern India and used extensively in South and Southeast Asia. Common names include moringa, drumstick tree (from the long, slender, triangular seed-pods), horseradish tree (from the taste of the roots, which resembles horseradish), ben tree (for its oil), or malunggay (as known in maritime or archipelagic areas in Asia). It is widely cultivated for its young seed pods and leaves, used as vegetables and for traditional herbal medicine. It is also used for water purification.
Malunggay.habitat_ecology: Found across various open fields, agricultural plots, and forest areas in the Philippines.
Malunggay.uses: Harvested for its highly nutritious leaves, commonly added to soups like Tinola, or utilized for its traditional herbal properties.

Malunggay___Healthy.symptoms: Lush compound leaflets with small white flowers, long hanging green seed pods.
Malunggay___Healthy.preventive_measures: Prune taller branches to maintain a bushy shrub height for easy harvesting.

Malunggay___Twig_Borer.symptoms: Dying twigs and shoots; sawdust-like powder at boreholes on branches; foliage turns yellow and falls.
Malunggay___Twig_Borer.organic_treatment: Prune off and burn affected branches. Spray neem oil on pruning wounds.
Malunggay___Twig_Borer.preventive_measures: Ensure proper tree spacing and clean orchard floor.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Requires constant humidity but well-draining soil)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Luya.description: Ginger (Zingiber officinale) is a flowering plant whose rhizome, ginger root or ginger, is widely used as a spice and a folk medicine. It is an herbaceous perennial that grows annual pseudostems (false stems made of the rolled bases of leaves) about one meter tall, bearing narrow leaf blades. The inflorescences bear flowers having pale yellow petals with purple edges, and arise directly from the rhizome on separate shoots. Ginger is in the family Zingiberaceae, which also includes turmeric (Curcuma longa), cardamom (Elettaria cardamomum), and galangal. Ginger originated in Maritime Southeast Asia and was likely domesticated first by the Austronesian peoples. It was transported with them throughout the Indo-Pacific during the Austronesian expansion (c. 5,000 BP), reaching as far as Hawaii. Ginger is one of the first spices to have been exported from Asia, arriving in Europe with the spice trade, and was used by ancient Greeks and Romans. The distantly related dicots in the genus Asarum are commonly called wild ginger because of their similar taste.
Luya.habitat_ecology: Found across various open fields, agricultural plots, and forest areas in the Philippines.
Luya.uses: Provides a warm, pungent flavor base for chicken soup (Tinola), fish stew (Paksiw), and is boiled to make traditional wellness ginger tea (Salabat).

Luya___Healthy.symptoms: Erect reed-like green stems, narrow leaves, healthy spicy ginger rhizomes underground.
Luya___Healthy.preventive_measures: Water regularly but keep soil extremely well-drained.

Luya___Bacterial_Wilt.symptoms: Sudden yellowing and wilting of shoots; ginger rhizomes turn brown, soft, and ooze foul fluid.
Luya___Bacterial_Wilt.organic_treatment: No cure. Immediately uproot and burn/bury infected clumps to isolate the soil.
Luya___Bacterial_Wilt.preventive_measures: Always plant disease-free ginger seed pieces. Rotate crops.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Requires trellis, water every 2-3 days)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Upo.description: Calabash (/ˈkæləbæʃ/; Lagenaria siceraria), also known as bottle gourd, white-flowered gourd, long melon, birdhouse gourd, New Guinea bean, New Guinea butter bean, Tasmania bean, zucca melon and opo squash, is a vine which is grown for its fruit. It belongs to the family Cucurbitaceae, is native to tropical Africa, and cultivated across the tropics. It can be either harvested young to be consumed as a vegetable, or harvested mature to be dried and used as a kitchen utensil (typically as a ladle or bowl), beverage container or a musical instrument. When it is fresh, the fruit has a light green smooth skin and white flesh. Calabash fruits have a variety of shapes: they can be huge and rounded, small and bottle-shaped, or slim and serpentine, and they can grow to be over a metre long. Rounder varieties are typically called calabash gourds (L. s. var. depressa) . Calabash gourds can grow to great size. One grown in Taylorsvlle, Kentucky in 2001 weighed 111.5 kg (246 lb). The gourd was one of the world's first cultivated plants grown not primarily for food, but for use as containers. The bottle gourd may have been carried from Asia to Africa, Europe, and the Americas in the course of human migration, or by seeds floating across the oceans inside the gourd. It has been proven to have been globally domesticated (and existed in the New World) during the Pre-Columbian era.
Upo.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Upo.uses: Commonly sautéed with ground pork or canned sardines (Ginisang Upo) to create an easy, budget-friendly everyday family meal.

Upo___Healthy.symptoms: Crawling vines with velvety heart-shaped leaves, white flowers, smooth green bottle gourds.
Upo___Healthy.preventive_measures: Ensure strong overhead trellis to bear the heavy weight of gourds.

Upo___Fruit_Fly.symptoms: Sunken brown holes on gourds oozing sap; gourds turn yellow and rot from the inside with small worms.
Upo___Fruit_Fly.organic_treatment: Bag young fruits in newspaper. Use yellow sticky pheromone cards.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Enjoys full sun; water twice a week)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Sigarilyas.description: The winged bean (Psophocarpus tetragonolobus), also known as cigarillas, Goa bean, four-angled bean, four-cornered bean, manila bean, princess bean, star bean, kamrangi bean, pea, or dragon bean, is a tropical herbaceous legume plant. Winged bean is widely recognised by consumers and farmers in South Asia and South East Asia for its variety of uses and disease resistance. Winged bean is nutrient-rich and all parts of the plant are edible. The leaves can be eaten like spinach, flowers can be used in salads, tubers can be eaten raw or cooked, and seeds can be used in similar ways as the soybean. The winged bean is an underutilised species but has the potential to become a major multi-use food crop in the tropics of Asia, Africa, and Latin America.
Sigarilyas.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Sigarilyas.uses: Its unique four-angled pods are valued for their crunchy texture, often cooked in coconut milk with chili (Ginataang Sigarilyas) or added to Pinakbet.

Sigarilyas___Healthy.symptoms: Climbing green vine with blue flowers and green four-winged bean pods.
Sigarilyas___Healthy.preventive_measures: Provide full sunlight and strong trellis netting.

Sigarilyas___Leaf_Spot.symptoms: Circular dark spots on leaves with yellow borders, causing premature leaf drying.
Sigarilyas___Leaf_Spot.organic_treatment: Prune affected leaves. Spray with dilute baking soda solution.
Sigarilyas___Leaf_Spot.preventive_measures: Avoid overhead watering; space plants to reduce moisture build-up.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate to High (Requires consistent moisture, likes cool weather)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Repolyo.description: Cabbage, comprising several cultivars of Brassica oleracea, is a leafy green, red (purple), or white (pale green) biennial plant grown as an annual vegetable crop for its dense-leaved heads. It is descended from the wild cabbage (B. oleracea var. oleracea), and belongs to the "cole crops" or brassicas, meaning it is closely related to broccoli and cauliflower (var. botrytis); Brussels sprouts (var. gemmifera); and Savoy cabbage (var. sabauda). A cabbage generally weighs between 500 and 1,000 grams (1 and 2 lb). Smooth-leafed, firm-headed green cabbages are the most common, with smooth-leafed purple cabbages and crinkle-leafed savoy cabbages of both colours being rarer. Under conditions of long sunny days, such as those found at high northern latitudes in summer, cabbages can grow quite large. As of 2012, the heaviest cabbage was 62.71 kilograms (138 lb 4 oz). Cabbage heads are generally picked during the first year of the plant's life cycle, but plants intended for seed are allowed to grow a second year and must be kept separate from other cole crops to prevent cross-pollination. Cabbage is prone to several nutrient deficiencies, as well as to multiple pests, and bacterial and fungal diseases.
Repolyo.habitat_ecology: Thrives in the cool, high-altitude climates of the Philippines, heavily cultivated in agricultural regions like Benguet (Baguio) and high-elevation zones of Mindanao.
Repolyo.uses: A highland staple shredded and used as a major component in stir-fried noodles (Pancit Canton/Bihon) or boiled in meaty stews like Nilaga.

Repolyo___Healthy.symptoms: Tight, firm cabbage head of overlapping blue-green thick crisp leaves.
Repolyo___Healthy.preventive_measures: Mulch soil to preserve moisture and cool root temperatures.

Repolyo___Black_Rot.symptoms: V-shaped yellow leaf margins; black veins; cabbage head becomes soft, black, and foul-smelling.
Repolyo___Black_Rot.organic_treatment: None. Remove and discard infected cabbages immediately.
Repolyo___Black_Rot.preventive_measures: Avoid overhead watering; enforce strict sanitation.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Keep soil damp; deep watering promotes straight roots)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Karot.description: The carrot (Daucus carota subsp. sativus) is a root vegetable in the umbellifer family Apiaceae. Carrots are typically orange in colour due to their beta-carotene content, which makes them a rich source of vitamin A. Heirloom variants may be white, yellow, red, purple, or black. They are biennial plants. The vegetable is a domesticated form of the wild carrot, native to Europe and Southwestern Asia. The domesticated plant probably originated in Iran where it was cultivated for its leaves and seeds. In 2022, world production of carrots (combined with turnips) topped 42 million tonnes, with China producing 44% of the total.
Karot.habitat_ecology: Thrives in the cool, high-altitude climates of the Philippines, heavily cultivated in agricultural regions like Benguet (Baguio) and high-elevation zones of Mindanao.
Karot.uses: Cultivated for food consumption, ornamental landscaping, or traditional agricultural uses.

Karot___Healthy.symptoms: Feathery green leaf clusters, healthy tapering orange carrot root swelling underground.
Karot___Healthy.preventive_measures: Ensure soil is completely loose and free of rocks to prevent crooked carrots.

Karot___Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Dark brown spots on leaf margins; leaf tips turn yellow, dry up, and die back, weakening carrot growth.
Karot___Leaf_Blight.organic_treatment: Prune damaged foliage. Spray with copper sulfate solution.
Karot___Leaf_Blight.preventive_measures: Water at the soil level; space out plantings.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water regularly at the base; avoid muddy leaves)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Baguio_Beans.description: Phaseolus vulgaris, the common bean, is a herbaceous annual plant. Its botanical classification, along with other Phaseolus species, is as a member of the legume family, Fabaceae. It forms a green-leaved vine which produces beans inside of pods. The common bean has a long history of cultivation. All wild members of the species have a climbing habit, but many cultivars are classified either as bush beans or climbing beans, depending on their style of growth. In 2022, 28 million tonnes of dry common beans were produced worldwide, led by India with 23% of the total.
Baguio_Beans.habitat_ecology: Thrives in the cool, high-altitude climates of the Philippines, heavily cultivated in agricultural regions like Benguet (Baguio) and high-elevation zones of Mindanao.
Baguio_Beans.uses: Regularly sautéed with garlic, onions, and carrots (Gisang Baguio Beans) or chopped into local vegetable egg rolls (Lumpiang Togue/Sariwa).

Baguio_Beans___Healthy.symptoms: Erect bushy structure or short climber, clean leaves, bunches of straight green bean pods.
Baguio_Beans___Healthy.preventive_measures: Ensure good drainage; avoid high nitrogen fertilizer.

Baguio_Beans___Anthracnose.symptoms: Dark brown sunken spots on bean pods and leaves; veins under leaves turn purple-black.
Baguio_Beans___Anthracnose.organic_treatment: Remove infected branches. Spray with copper sulfate solution or neem wash.
Baguio_Beans___Anthracnose.preventive_measures: Only save seeds from disease-free bean pods.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Drought-resistant once established, prefers full sun)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Kundol.description: Benincasa hispida, the wax gourd, also called ash gourd, white gourd, winter gourd, winter melon, tallow gourd, ash pumpkin, dongah or Chinese preserving melon, is a species of vine. The wax gourd is native to South and Southeast Asia. It is grown, especially in Asia, for its very large fruit, eaten as a vegetable when mature. One variety of the plant, called chi qua (Benincasa hispida var. chieh-qua), is commonly used in Asian cuisine.
Kundol.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Kundol.uses: Traditionally candied into sweet preserves (Hopia Kundol filling) or simmered in comforting chicken broths and Chinese-Filipino soups.

Kundol___Healthy.symptoms: Climbing fuzzy green vines, yellow flowers, large oblong gourds covered in a powdery white wax layer.
Kundol___Healthy.preventive_measures: Ensure strong trellises to support the heavy wax gourds.

Kundol___Downy_Mildew.symptoms: Angular yellow spots on leaves; gray fuzzy mold layer underneath leaves in damp weather.
Kundol___Downy_Mildew.organic_treatment: Prune old lower foliage. Spray wood ash mixture or baking soda spray.
Kundol___Downy_Mildew.preventive_measures: Expose plot to full sun; avoid dense planting.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Requires trellis, water when soil dries)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Bataw.description: Lablab purpureus is a species of bean in the family Fabaceae. It is native to sub-Saharan Africa and it is cultivated throughout the tropics for food. English language common names include hyacinth bean, lablab-bean bonavist bean/pea, dolichos bean, seim or sem bean, lablab bean, Egyptian kidney bean, Indian bean, bataw and Australian pea. Lablab is a monotypic genus. The name lablab which is also capitalized as its genus name is given by Robert Sweet from the previous name of Dolichos lablab by Carl Linnaeus, its epithet comes from Arabic: لَبْلَاب, romanized: lablāb.
Bataw.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Bataw.uses: A classic Bahay Kubo legume whose flat pods are added to regional boiled vegetable dishes like Dinengdeng or Pinakbet.

Bataw___Healthy.symptoms: Creeping green vines, purple or white flower clusters, purple-bordered flat green bean pods.
Bataw___Healthy.preventive_measures: Prune regularly to control vine spreading.

Bataw___Pod_Borer.symptoms: Small holes in bean pods; pods contain fecal deposits inside; seeds are eaten; pods rot.
Bataw___Pod_Borer.organic_treatment: Prune and destroy bored pods. Spray with chili-garlic extract or soap-water solution.

watering_needs.frequency: Low to Moderate (Highly resilient to heat and drought)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Patani.description: A lima bean (Phaseolus lunatus), also commonly known as butter bean, sieva bean, double bean or Madagascar bean, is a legume grown for its edible seeds or beans. Although lima beans and butter beans are the same species, they are sometimes considered distinct in culinary use, the former being small and green, the latter large and yellow. In areas where both are considered to be lima beans, the green variety may be labeled as "baby" (and less commonly "junior") limas.
Patani.habitat_ecology: Commonly cultivated in lowland backyard gardens, open fields, and household plots throughout the Philippines.
Patani.uses: Its starchy seeds are boiled and added to traditional vegetable medleys like Bulanglang, or cooked with coconut milk.

Patani___Healthy.symptoms: Resilient crawling vines, white-green flowers, broad flat green pods containing crescent-shaped beans.
Patani___Healthy.preventive_measures: Very drought-resistant; water sparingly once established.

Patani___Pod_Borer.symptoms: Entry holes on pods; internal seeds are consumed; pods exhibit mold and drop early.
Patani___Pod_Borer.organic_treatment: Harvest and destroy infested pods. Spray with neem extract.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water young trees regularly; mature trees are drought-tolerant)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Mangga.description: A mango is an edible stone fruit produced by the tropical tree Mangifera indica. It originated in the northeastern part of the Indian subcontinent, in what is now Bangladesh, northeastern India and Myanmar. M. indica has been cultivated in South and Southeast Asia since ancient times, resulting in two modern mango cultivar lineages: the "Indian" and the "Southeast Asian" types. Other species in the genus Mangifera also produce edible fruits called "mangoes," most of which are found in the Malesian ecoregion. There are several hundred cultivars of mango worldwide. Depending on the cultivar, mango fruit varies in size, shape, sweetness, skin colour, and flesh colour, which may be pale yellow, gold, green, or orange. Mango is the national fruit of India, Pakistan, and the Philippines, while the mango tree is the national tree of Bangladesh.
Mangga.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Mangga.uses: Eaten ripe as a sweet dessert, blended into shakes, or eaten unripe and sour (Green Mango) with salty shrimp paste (Bagoong).

Mangga___Healthy.symptoms: Clean, lanceolate dark green leaves; dense green canopy; clean maturing mangoes.
Mangga___Healthy.preventive_measures: Prune inner branches to let sunlight sterilize the tree center.

Mangga___Anthracnose.symptoms: Black spots on leaves and flowers; developing mangoes exhibit dark sunken lesions and fall early.
Mangga___Anthracnose.organic_treatment: Prune affected shoots. Spray copper fungicide before flowering. Perform hot water treatment on harvested fruit.
Mangga___Anthracnose.preventive_measures: Maintain orchard hygiene by removing fallen leaves and twigs.

watering_needs.frequency: High (Needs abundant water; water daily in dry season)
watering_needs.description: Requires consistent and abundant soil moisture. Highly susceptible to drying out; water daily and protect from extreme dry spells.

Saging.description: A banana is an elongated, edible fruit that is botanically a berry produced by several kinds of large treelike herbaceous flowering plants in the genus Musa. In some countries, cooking bananas are called plantains, distinguishing them from dessert bananas. The fruit is variable in size, color and firmness, but is usually elongated and curved, with soft flesh rich in starch covered with a peel, which may have a variety of colors when ripe. It grows upward in clusters near the top of the plant. Almost all modern edible seedless (parthenocarp) cultivated bananas come from two wild species – Musa acuminata and Musa balbisiana, or their hybrids. Musa species are native to tropical Indomalaya and Australia; they were probably domesticated in New Guinea. They are grown in 135 countries, primarily for their fruit, and to a lesser extent to make banana paper and textiles, while some are grown as ornamental plants. The world's largest producers of bananas in 2022 were India and China, which together accounted for approximately 26% of total production. Bananas are eaten raw or cooked in recipes varying from curries to banana chips, fritters, fruit preserves, or simply baked or steamed.
Saging.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Saging.uses: Eaten fresh as a table fruit, or cooked (particularly the Saba variety) into street foods like Banana Cue, Turon, or sweetened Minatamis na Saging.

Saging___Healthy.symptoms: Broad erect green leaves, thick pseudostem, clean banana bunch with banana heart.
Saging___Healthy.preventive_measures: Provide potassium-rich organic fertilizers like wood ash.

Saging___Panama_Disease.condition_name: Panama Disease / Fusarium Wilt (Lanta sa Saging)
Saging___Panama_Disease.symptoms: Yellowing of leaf margins; leaves bend down and hang like a skirt; pseudostem splits at base.
Saging___Panama_Disease.organic_treatment: No cure. Destroy infected plant and isolate the spot. Do not move suckers.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water moderately; sensitive to root rot if waterlogged)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Papaya.description: The papaya (/pəˈpaɪə/, US: /pəˈpɑːjə/) is the plant species Carica papaya, one of the 21 accepted species in the genus Carica of the family Caricaceae. It was first domesticated in Mesoamerica, within modern-day southern Mexico and Central America. It is grown in several countries in regions with a tropical climate. In 2024, India was the leading producer, accounting for 36% of the world total.
Papaya.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Papaya.uses: Unripe green papaya is shredded for pickled Atchara or added to Tinola soup; ripe papaya is eaten fresh as a sweet, digestive-friendly breakfast fruit.

Papaya___Healthy.symptoms: Crown of large lobed leaves; clean trunk with clusters of green/yellow fruits.
Papaya___Healthy.preventive_measures: Ensure soil is highly draining to prevent root rot.

Papaya___Ring_Spot.symptoms: Yellow mosaic patches on leaves; dark green oil-spots on trunk; circular rings on papaya fruits.
Papaya___Ring_Spot.organic_treatment: No organic cure. Uproot and burn infected trees immediately to contain the virus.
Papaya___Ring_Spot.preventive_measures: Control aphid vectors using soapy water spray.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Highly adaptable, prefers sandy loam with high humidity)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Niyog.description: The coconut (Cocos nucifera) is a member of the palm family (Arecaceae) and the only living species of the genus Cocos. The term "coconut" (or the archaic "cocoanut") can denote the whole coconut palm tree or the large hard fruit. Originally native to the Central Indo-Pacific, they are ubiquitous in coastal tropical regions. The coconut tree provides food, fuel, cosmetics, folk medicine and building materials. The inner flesh of the mature fruit forms a regular part of the diets of many people in the tropics and subtropics. Coconut endosperm contains a large quantity of a liquid, "coconut water". Mature coconuts can be processed for oil and coconut milk from the flesh, charcoal from the hard shell, and coir from the fibrous husk. Dried coconut flesh is called copra, and the oil and milk derived from it are commonly used in cooking and in soaps and cosmetics. Sweet coconut sap can be made into drinks or fermented into palm wine or coconut vinegar. The hard shells, fibrous husks and long pinnate leaves are used to make products for furnishing and decoration.
Niyog.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Niyog.uses: Grated meat is squeezed for coconut milk (Gata) used in savory stews and desserts, while coconut water (Buko) is served as a refreshing beverage.

Niyog___Healthy.symptoms: Lush green crown of fronds; trunk free of damage; heavy bunches of coconuts.
Niyog___Healthy.preventive_measures: Clean the crown of dead leaves and debris regularly.

Niyog___Cocolisap.symptoms: Leaves turn yellow and dry up; undersides of fronds are covered in tiny yellow/white scales.
Niyog___Cocolisap.organic_treatment: Prune and burn heavily infested fronds. Spray with soap and coconut oil mixture.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water regularly, especially during fruit production)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Calamansi.description: Calamansi (Citrus × microcarpa), also known as calamondin, Philippine lime, or Philippine lemon, is a citrus hybrid cultivated predominantly in the Philippines. It is native to the Philippines, parts of Indonesia (Borneo, Sumatra, and Sulawesi), Malaysia, and Brunei, as well as Taiwan, Sri Lanka and parts of southern China. Calamansi is ubiquitous in traditional Philippine cuisine. It is naturally very sour, and is used in various condiments, beverages, dishes, marinades, and preserves. Calamansi is also used as an ingredient in Malaysian and Indonesian cuisines.
Calamansi.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Calamansi.uses: Its sour juice is squeezed over noodles, grilled meats, and used as a base for dipping sauces (Toyomansi) or hot wellness tea.

Calamansi___Healthy.symptoms: Shiny green leaves, white fragrant flowers, juicy green spherical citrus fruits.
Calamansi___Healthy.preventive_measures: Apply organic compost around the root zone twice a year.

Calamansi___Citrus_Canker.symptoms: Raised brown corky spots with yellow halos on leaves, stems, and fruits.
Calamansi___Citrus_Canker.organic_treatment: Prune infected twigs. Spray copper fungicide or baking soda wash.
Calamansi___Citrus_Canker.preventive_measures: Plant windbreaks around the trees to reduce bacterial spread via wind/rain.

watering_needs.frequency: Low (Drought-tolerant bromeliad, water once a week)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Pinya.description: The pineapple (Ananas comosus) is a tropical plant with an edible fruit; it is the most economically significant plant in the family Bromeliaceae. The pineapple is native to South America, where it has been cultivated for many centuries. The introduction of the pineapple plant to Europe in the 17th century made it a significant cultural icon of luxury. Since the 1820s, pineapple has been commercially grown in greenhouses and many tropical plantations. The fruit, particularly its juice, has diverse uses in cuisines and desserts.
Pinya.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Pinya.uses: Slices are eaten fresh, processed into canned desserts, or added as a sweet-tangy component in savory dishes like Hamonado and Sweet and Sour Pork.

Pinya___Healthy.symptoms: Rosette of stiff spiny green leaves; single central pine fruit developing normally.

Pinya___Heart_Rot.symptoms: Central leaves turn yellow-brown and are easily pulled out from the plant; rot smells foul.
Pinya___Heart_Rot.organic_treatment: Uproot and burn. Do not plant pinya in that wet soil again.
Pinya___Heart_Rot.preventive_measures: Dip slips/cuttings in organic copper solution before planting.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Keep soil moist but well-drained)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Guyabano.description: Soursop (also called graviola, guyabano, and in Hispanic America guanábana) is the fruit of Annona muricata, a broadleaf, flowering, evergreen tree. It is native to the tropical Americas and is widely propagated. It is in the same genus, Annona, as cherimoya and is in the Annonaceae family. The soursop is adapted to areas of high humidity and relatively warm winters; temperatures below 5 °C (41 °F) will cause damage to leaves and small branches, and temperatures below 3 °C (37 °F) can be fatal. The fruit becomes dry and is no longer good for concentrate.
Guyabano.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Guyabano.uses: Its fibrous, creamy white pulp is eaten fresh, squeezed into juice, or processed into purees, valued locally for its traditional cancer-fighting claims.

Guyabano___Healthy.symptoms: Glossy green oval leaves, clean bark, spiky green soursop fruits.
Guyabano___Healthy.preventive_measures: Keep orchard clean of fallen leaves.

Guyabano___Anthracnose.symptoms: Black spots on leaves and flowers; guyabano fruits rot, turn black, and mummify.
Guyabano___Anthracnose.organic_treatment: Prune infected parts. Spray with neem oil or organic copper wash.
Guyabano___Anthracnose.preventive_measures: Ensure trees have adequate spacing to minimize humidity.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water regularly when young, drought-resistant when mature)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Langka.description: The jackfruit, jakfruit or nangka (Artocarpus heterophyllus) is a species of tree in the fig, mulberry, and breadfruit family (Moraceae). The jackfruit is the largest tree fruit, reaching as much as 55 kg (120 pounds) in weight, 90 cm (35 inches) in length, and 50 cm (20 inches) in diameter. A mature jackfruit tree produces some 200 fruits per year, with older trees bearing up to 500 fruits in a year. The jackfruit is a multiple fruit composed of hundreds to thousands of individual flowers; the fleshy petals of the unripe fruit are eaten by humans, in addition to the ripened fruit.
Langka.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Langka.uses: Ripe yellow bulbs are added to sweeten Turon or Minatamis; unripe green langka is simmered in coconut milk with salted fish (Ginataang Langka).

Langka___Healthy.symptoms: Thick green leathery leaves, huge spiky jackfruits directly on the trunk.
Langka___Healthy.preventive_measures: Bag the developing fruits early in large sacks to prevent fruit flies.

Langka___Fruit_Rot.symptoms: Soft, water-soaked spots on fruits; fruits cover with a dark furry mold and drop.
Langka___Fruit_Rot.organic_treatment: Remove and bury/burn infected fruits. Spray with copper sulfate solution.
Langka___Fruit_Rot.preventive_measures: Bag fruits early. Prune lower twigs to improve airflow.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Requires dry spells to induce flowering, water normal times)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Santol.description: Sandoricum koetjape, the santol, sentul, setun or cotton fruit, is a tropical fruit native to maritime Southeast Asia. The santol is native to Malesia and New Guinea. It has been introduced to Indochina, Sri Lanka, India, northern Australia, Mauritius, and Seychelles. It is commonly cultivated throughout these regions and the fruits are seasonally abundant in the local and international markets.
Santol.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Santol.uses: Eaten fresh with salt; the thick rind is also grated and simmered in coconut milk with chilies and pork to make the spicy dish Sinantolan.

Santol___Healthy.symptoms: Large compound green leaves, clean bark, smooth yellow-brown globose fruits.
Santol___Healthy.preventive_measures: Clean up fallen leaves to control pest breeding.

Santol___Gall_Mite.symptoms: Leaf surface is covered in numerous raised green/red wart-like bumps (galls).
Santol___Gall_Mite.organic_treatment: Prune heavily infested leaves and burn them. Spray with sulfur solution or neem oil.
Santol___Gall_Mite.preventive_measures: Inspect new leaf flushes for mites.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Highly drought-resistant but benefits from weekly water)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Chico.description: Manilkara zapota, commonly known as sapodilla, sapote, chicozapote, chico, chicoo, chicle, naseberry, nispero, or soapapple, among other names, is an evergreen tree native to southern Mexico and Central America. An example natural occurrence is in coastal Yucatán, in the Petenes mangroves ecoregion, where it is a subdominant plant species. It was introduced to the Philippines during Spanish colonization. It is grown in large quantities in Mexico and in tropical Asia, including India, Pakistan, Thailand, Malaysia, Cambodia, Indonesia, Vietnam, Bangladesh, as well as in the Caribbean. Most of the common names of Manilkara zapota like "sapodilla", "chiku", and "chicozapote" come from Spanish meaning "little sapote". Other common names in English include bully tree, soapapple tree, sawo, marmalade plum and dilly tree.
Chico.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Chico.uses: Eaten fresh as a sweet, brown-skinned dessert fruit, prized for its grain-textured, brown sugar-like honeyed flavor.

Chico___Healthy.symptoms: Oval glossy leaves, brown scaly chico fruits growing in clusters.
Chico___Healthy.preventive_measures: Water regularly during fruiting to ensure juicy fruit pulp.

Chico___Mealybugs.symptoms: White cotton-like clusters on fruit stems and under leaves; black sooty mold follows.
Chico___Mealybugs.organic_treatment: Spray with soapy water or neem oil. Blast with high-pressure water.
Chico___Mealybugs.preventive_measures: Control ants (ants farm and protect mealybugs).

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Requires high moisture and regular watering)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Rambutan.description: Rambutan (/ræmˈbuːtən/; Nephelium lappaceum) is a medium-sized tropical tree in the family Sapindaceae. The name also refers to the edible fruit produced by this tree. The rambutan is native to Southeast Asia. It is closely related to several other edible tropical fruits, including the lychee, longan, pulasan, and quenepa. It is an evergreen tree growing to a height of 15–24 m (49–79 ft). The leaves are alternate, 14–30 cm (6–12 in) long, pinnate, with three to eleven leaflets, each leaflet 5–15 cm (2–6 in) wide and 3–10 cm (1.2–3.9 in) broad with an entire margin.
Rambutan.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Rambutan.uses: Peeled and eaten fresh as a seasonal table fruit, enjoyed for its sweet, translucent, juicy aril surrounding a single seed.

Rambutan___Healthy.symptoms: Clean green leaves; clusters of bright red hairy fruits growing at branch tips.
Rambutan___Healthy.preventive_measures: Conduct post-harvest pruning to encourage new fruiting shoots.

Rambutan___Powdery_Mildew.symptoms: White powdery growth on flowers and young fruit clusters, causing them to turn black and drop.
Rambutan___Powdery_Mildew.organic_treatment: Prune shaded inner twigs. Spray with sulfur powder or baking soda wash.
Rambutan___Powdery_Mildew.preventive_measures: Ensure tree canopy gets full sunlight.

watering_needs.frequency: High (Sensitive to dry conditions, requires regular watering)
watering_needs.description: Requires consistent and abundant soil moisture. Highly susceptible to drying out; water daily and protect from extreme dry spells.

Lanzones.description: Lanzones (Lansium domesticum) is a species of tree in the mahogany family, cultivated for its edible fruit. The fruits grow in grape-like clusters, featuring a thin, yellow leathery skin that releases a milky sap when peeled. The translucent, sweet, and tangy flesh is divided into segments, some of which contain bitter seeds. It is a highly popular seasonal fruit tree grown throughout the Philippines.
Lanzones.habitat_ecology: Thrives in warm, humid, and rain-abundant tropical climates, commonly grown in the southern regions of the Philippines, particularly in Davao and Mindanao.
Lanzones.uses: Its segments are peeled and eaten fresh as a prized seasonal delicacy; the dried skin is traditionally burned to repel mosquitoes.

Lanzones___Healthy.symptoms: Erect trunk, bunches of yellow-round fruits hanging on branches.
Lanzones___Healthy.preventive_measures: Protect trees from dry winds; mulching roots helps preserve moisture.

Lanzones___Bark_Borer.symptoms: Small holes in the bark; sawdust-like frass on the trunk; branches die back.
Lanzones___Bark_Borer.organic_treatment: Scrape infested bark area to destroy larvae. Apply neem oil paste.
Lanzones___Bark_Borer.preventive_measures: Maintain tree vigor with regular compost feeding.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Very hardy, drought-tolerant once established)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Duhat.description: Syzygium cumini, also known as Java plum, Malabar plum, black plum, jamun, jaman, jambul, jambu, or jambolan, is an evergreen tropical tree in the flowering plant family Myrtaceae, and favored for its fruit, timber, and ornamental value. It is native to the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It can reach heights of up to 30 m (100 ft) and can live more than 100 years. A rapidly growing plant, it is considered an invasive species in many world regions.
