Syzygium cumini has been introduced to areas including islands of the Pacific and Indian Oceans, Australia, Hong Kong and Singapore.
Duhat.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Duhat.uses: Usually shaken in a bowl with rock salt (Duhat shake) to soften the astringency before eating, or fermented into local plum wines.

Duhat___Healthy.symptoms: Broad glossy green leaves, clusters of dark purple to black oval berries.
Duhat___Healthy.preventive_measures: Harvest ripe fruits daily to avoid attracting pests.

Duhat___Leaf_Gall.symptoms: Small green and yellow blisters on leaf surface; leaves deform and dry.
Duhat___Leaf_Gall.organic_treatment: Prune and destroy infected leaves. Spray with neem oil.
Duhat___Leaf_Gall.preventive_measures: Prune internal canopy to let in sunlight.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Highly adaptable, grows almost anywhere)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Bayabas.description: Guava (/ˈɡwɑːvə/ GWAH-və), also known as the 'guava-pear' in various regions, is a common tropical fruit cultivated in many tropical and subtropical regions. The common guava Psidium guajava (lemon guava, apple guava) is a small tree in the myrtle family (Myrtaceae), native to Mexico, Central America, the Caribbean and northern South America. Botanically, guavas are berries. The name guava is also given to some other species in the genus Psidium, such as strawberry guava (Psidium cattleyanum) and to the pineapple guava, Feijoa sellowiana. In 2024, the world production of guavas (data combined with mangos and mangosteens) was 62 million tonnes, led by India having 45% of the total.
Bayabas.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Bayabas.uses: Eaten fresh when ripe, or used traditionally as a natural souring agent in fish/pork broth (Sinigang sa Bayabas) and for antiseptic leaf washes.

Bayabas___Healthy.symptoms: Smooth peeling bark, green ribbed leaves, firm green round guava fruits.
Bayabas___Healthy.preventive_measures: Bag the guavas early to prevent fruit fly entry.

Bayabas___Whitefly.symptoms: Underside of leaves covered in small white insects and sticky honeydew; black mold on leaves.
Bayabas___Whitefly.organic_treatment: Spray with soap-water mixture or neem oil wash.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Drought-tolerant once mature)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Kaimito.description: Chrysophyllum cainito is a tropical tree of the family Sapotaceae. It is native to the Isthmus of Panama, where it was domesticated. It has spread to the Greater Antilles and the West Indies and is now grown throughout the tropics, including Southeast Asia. It grows rapidly and reaches 20 meters in height. The common names cainito and caimito likely come from the Mayan words cab (juice), im (breast), and vitis (sap), via Spanish. Star apple is a common name. It is also known as the purple apple.
Kaimito.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Kaimito.uses: Chilled and eaten fresh by scooping out the sweet, milky, gelatinous flesh, often mixed with condensed milk or cream.

Kaimito___Healthy.symptoms: Leaves with green tops and bronze golden undersides; firm purple star apples.
Kaimito___Healthy.preventive_measures: Prune lowest branches to allow air circulation near the base.

Kaimito___Fruit_Fly.symptoms: Sunken soft patches on star apple skin; white maggots inside the sweet pulp, causing early rot.
Kaimito___Fruit_Fly.organic_treatment: Bag fruits in paper. Hang methyl eugenol traps around the tree.
Kaimito___Fruit_Fly.preventive_measures: Gather and dispose of all fallen fruits.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Prefers evenly moist soil)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Kamias.description: Averrhoa bilimbi (commonly known as bilimbi, orkkaappuli, cucumber tree, or tree sorrel) is a fruit-bearing tree of the genus Averrhoa, family Oxalidaceae. It is native to the Maluku Islands and Sulawesi in Indonesia, and has become naturalised and is common throughout Southeast Asia. It is cultivated in parts of tropical South Asia and the Americas. It bears edible but extremely sour fruit. It is a close relative of the carambola tree. Averrhoa bilimbi is a small tropical tree reaching up to 15 m in height. It is often multitrunked, quickly dividing into ramifications. Bilimbi leaves are alternate, pinnate, measuring approximately 30–60 cm in length. Each leaf contains 11-37 leaflets; ovate to oblong, 2–10 cm long, and 1–2 cm wide, and cluster at branch extremities. The leaves are quite similar to those of the Otaheite gooseberry. The tree is cauliflorous with 18–68 flowers in panicles that form on the trunk and other branches. The flowers are heterostylous, borne in a pendulous panicle inflorescence. The flower is fragrant, corolla of 5 petals 10–30 mm long, yellowish green to reddish purple.
Kamias.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Kamias.uses: Extremely sour fruit used fresh as a powerful acid/souring agent in stews (Paksiw, Sinigang), or sun-dried to preserve it for cooking.

Kamias___Healthy.symptoms: Clusters of highly sour green bilimbi fruits growing directly on trunk.

Kamias___Sooty_Mold.symptoms: Leaves and fruits get covered in a thin, dry black powder.
Kamias___Sooty_Mold.organic_treatment: Spray with soapy water to wash off the mold. Control sucking insects.
Kamias___Sooty_Mold.preventive_measures: Keep canopy open to direct sunlight.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Keep soil moist but not soggy)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Balimbing.description: Averrhoa carambola is a species of tree in the family Oxalidaceae native to tropical Southeast Asia; it has a number of common names, including carambola, star fruit and five-corner. It is a small tree or shrub that grows 5 to 12 m (16 to 39 ft) tall, with rose to red-purple flowers. The flowers are small and bell-shaped, with five petals that have whitish edges. The flowers are often produced year round under tropical conditions. The tree is cultivated in tropical and semitropical regions for its edible fruits. The species in the genus Averrhoa along with the species in genus Sarcotheca are the only woody, tree-like plants belonging to the Oxalis family Oxalidaceae. The Oxalis family has nearly 900 species; most are herbaceous perennials or annuals native from tropical and semitropical locations, although a number also grow in other parts of the world. Averrhoa has sometimes been placed in the family Averrhoaceae. Averrhoa carambola is one of two species in the genus Averrhoa, both have edible fruit; the other species A. bilimbi, which is sometimes called the bilimbi or the cucumber tree, is limited to tropical regions. The fruits of A. bilimbi are too sour to be eaten raw, while the sweet forms of A. carambola are eaten raw. The fruits of A. bilimbi and the sour forms—which have high oxalic acid content—of A. carambola are pickled and made into jelly, jam, and juice.
Balimbing.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Balimbing.uses: Sliced crosswise to reveal star shapes, eaten fresh with salt for a crunchy, juicy snack, or juiced as a thirst-quenching beverage.

Balimbing___Healthy.preventive_measures: Bag the star fruits with paper bags early.

Balimbing___Fruit_Fly.symptoms: Tiny puncture spots; fruit flesh becomes soft, turns brown, and is filled with maggots.
Balimbing___Fruit_Fly.organic_treatment: Bag fruits when they are 1-inch long. Clean up all fallen fruit.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Regular watering; protect from soil sogginess)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Suha.description: The pomelo (/ˈpɒmɪloʊ, ˈpʌm-/ POM-il-oh, PUM-; or pummelo, Citrus maxima), also known as a shaddock, is the largest citrus fruit. It is an ancestor of several cultivated citrus species, including the bitter orange and the grapefruit. It is a natural, non-hybrid citrus fruit, native to Southeast Asia. Similar in taste to a sweet grapefruit, the pomelo is commonly eaten and used for festive occasions throughout Southeast and East Asia. As with the grapefruit, phytochemicals in the pomelo have the potential for drug interactions. The pomelo tree can be 5–15 metres (16–50 feet) tall, with a trunk, often rather crooked, that is 10–30 centimetres (4–12 inches) thick, and low-hanging, irregular branches. The petioles (leaf stalks) are distinctly winged. The leaves are alternate, ovate or elliptic in shape, and 5–20 cm (2–8 in) long; they are leathery and dull green above, hairy beneath. The flowers, produced singly or in clusters, are fragrant and yellow-white.
Suha.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Suha.uses: Peeled and eaten fresh by the segment as a refreshing citrus snack, or tossed into local green salads and fruit punches.

Suha___Healthy.symptoms: Large winged green leaves, huge green/yellow thick-skinned pomelos.
Suha___Healthy.preventive_measures: Fertilize with compost twice a year.

Suha___Citrus_Canker.symptoms: Raised brown craters with yellow halos on leaves and fruit skin.
Suha___Citrus_Canker.organic_treatment: Prune all diseased twigs. Spray with copper sulfate solution.
Suha___Citrus_Canker.preventive_measures: Grow windbreaks; spray copper during leaf flushes.

watering_needs.frequency: Low to Moderate (Drought-tolerant, hates wet roots)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Atis.description: Annona squamosa is a small, well-branched tree or shrub from the family Annonaceae that bears edible fruits called sugar apples or sweetsops or custard apples. It tolerates a tropical lowland climate better than its relatives Annona reticulata and Annona cherimola (whose fruits often share the same name) helping make it the most widely cultivated of these species. Annona squamosa is semi-(or late) deciduous, and 3 to 8 metres (10 to 26 feet) tall, similar to soursop (Annona muricata). It is a native of tropical climate in the Americas, and Spanish traders aboard the Manila galleons docking in the Philippines brought it to Asia.
Atis.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Atis.uses: Eaten fresh by pressing the soft, scaly skin open and consuming the sweet, custardy white segments around the black seeds.

Atis___Healthy.symptoms: Light green leaves, bumpy green sugar apples developing normally.
Atis___Healthy.preventive_measures: Expose to full sun and well-draining soil.

Atis___Mealybugs.symptoms: White powdery clusters cover the crevices of the fruit; fruit stops growing and turns black.
Atis___Mealybugs.organic_treatment: Spray soap solution or neem oil. Use high-pressure water spray to dislodge clusters.
Atis___Mealybugs.preventive_measures: Deter ant colonizations around the base.

watering_needs.frequency: High (Water frequently for juicy fruits)
watering_needs.description: Requires consistent and abundant soil moisture. Highly susceptible to drying out; water daily and protect from extreme dry spells.

Makopa.description: Syzygium samarangense is a species of flowering plant in the family Myrtaceae, native to an area that includes the Greater Sunda Islands, Malay Peninsula, and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, but introduced in prehistoric times to a wider area and now widely cultivated in the tropics. Common names in English include wax apple, Java apple, Semarang rose-apple, and wax jambu. Syzygium samarangense is a tropical tree growing to 12 metres (39 feet) tall, with evergreen leaves 10–25 cm (4–10 in) long and 5–10 cm (2–4 in) broad. The leaves are elliptical, but rounded at the base; they are aromatic when crushed. The trunk is relatively short, with a wide – yet open – crown starting low on the tree. The bark is pinkish-gray in color, and flakes readily.
Makopa.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Makopa.uses: Eaten fresh as a crisp, mildly sweet, bell-shaped fruit, often dipped in rock salt for a refreshing afternoon snack.

Makopa___Healthy.symptoms: Dense green leaves, bunches of bell-shaped pink/red wax apples.
Makopa___Healthy.preventive_measures: Water regularly to encourage crunchy and juicy fruits.

Makopa___Fruit_Fly.symptoms: Tiny puncture wounds on the red skin; fruit becomes soft and rots from within.
Makopa___Fruit_Fly.organic_treatment: Bag fruit clusters. Collect and bury rotting fallen fruits.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate to High (Water regularly until fruit reaches full size; reduce water before harvest for sweetness)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Pakwan.description: The watermelon (Citrullus lanatus) is a species of flowering plant in the family Cucurbitaceae, that has a large, edible fruit. It is a scrambling and trailing vine-like plant, and is widely cultivated worldwide, with more than 1,000 varieties. Watermelons are grown in favorable climates from tropical to temperate regions worldwide for its large edible fruit, which is a berry with a hard rind and no internal divisions, botanically called a pepo. The sweet, juicy flesh is usually deep red to pink, with many seeds that are black when mature, white when immature. Seedless varieties also exist. The fruit can be eaten raw or pickled, and the rind is edible after cooking. It may also be consumed as a juice or an ingredient in mixed beverages.
Pakwan.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Pakwan.uses: Slices are served chilled as a sweet, hydrating dessert, or blended into fruit shakes during the hot summer months.

Pakwan___Healthy.symptoms: Vigorous trailing vines, yellow flowers, large glossy striped watermelons.
Pakwan___Healthy.preventive_measures: Rotate crops and protect growing fruits from soil contact by resting them on straw.

Pakwan___Fusarium_Wilt.symptoms: Vines wilt during the heat of the day and recover at night; eventually, the entire vine collapses and turns brown.
Pakwan___Fusarium_Wilt.organic_treatment: None. Remove and burn the wilted vines. Apply wood ash to raise soil pH.
Pakwan___Fusarium_Wilt.preventive_measures: Grow in well-drained soil; practice a 5-year crop rotation.

Pakwan___Anthracnose.symptoms: Water-soaked spots on leaves that turn black; circular sunken black lesions on watermelon rinds.
Pakwan___Anthracnose.organic_treatment: Remove diseased leaves. Spray copper sulfate solution or neem oil.
Pakwan___Anthracnose.preventive_measures: Avoid overhead watering; use clean seed.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Dewater regularly but ensure excellent soil drainage to prevent root rot)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Avocado.description: The avocado, alligator pear or avocado pear (Persea americana) is an evergreen tree in the laurel family (Lauraceae). It is native to the Americas, with archaeological evidence of early human avocado use dating back thousands of years across various regions of Central and South America. It was prized for its large and unusually oily fruit. The native range of avocado (Persea americana) extends from Mexico to Peru, encompassing much of Central America and parts of northern and western South America. Its fruit, sometimes also referred to as an alligator pear or avocado pear, is botanically a large berry containing a single large seed. Sequencing of its genome showed that the evolution of avocados was shaped by polyploidy events and that commercial varieties have a hybrid origin. Avocado trees are partly self-pollinating, and are often propagated through grafting to maintain consistent fruit output. Avocados are presently cultivated in the tropical and Mediterranean climates of many countries. As of 2023, Mexico is the world's leading producer of avocados, supplying 29% of the global harvest of 10.5 million tonnes.
Avocado.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Avocado.uses: Eaten as a dessert in the Philippines, mashed with condensed milk or sugar and milk, or blended with ice to make a rich, creamy shake.

Avocado___Healthy.symptoms: Lush dark green leaves, straight trunk, firm pear-shaped green avocadoes.
Avocado___Healthy.preventive_measures: Plant in sandy loam mounds to ensure roots never sit in stagnant water.

Avocado___Root_Rot.symptoms: Foliage turns pale green and wilts; leaves drop leaving a bare canopy; roots turn black and mushy.
Avocado___Root_Rot.organic_treatment: None. Pull out dying trees. Apply generous amounts of compost and gypsum to remaining trees to suppress the fungus.
Avocado___Root_Rot.preventive_measures: Strictly avoid waterlogging. Use resistant rootstocks.

Avocado___Anthracnose.symptoms: Circular brown-black spots on avocado skin; spots expand and sink into the flesh, causing decay.
Avocado___Anthracnose.organic_treatment: Harvest fruits early before spots worsen. Spray copper fungicide before fruiting.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate to High (Needs consistent moisture during fruit set; sensitive to flooding)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Durian.description: The durian (/ˈdʊəriən/ ) is the edible fruit of several tree species belonging to the genus Durio. There are 30 recognised species, at least nine of which produce edible fruit. Durio zibethinus, native to Borneo, Sumatra, and the Malay Peninsula, is the only species available on the international market. It has over 300 named varieties in Thailand and over 200 in Malaysia as of 2021. Other species are sold in their local regions. Known in some regions as the "king of fruits", the durian is distinctive for its large size, strong odour, and thorn-covered rind. The fruit can grow as large as 30 cm (12 in) long and 15 cm (6 in) in diameter, and it typically weighs 1 to 3 kg (2 to 7 lb). Its shape ranges from oblong to round, the colour of its husk from green to brown, and its flesh from pale yellow to red, depending on the species. The flavour of the flesh is complex with savoury notes. The texture is creamy and pudding-like.
Durian.habitat_ecology: Thrives in warm, humid, and rain-abundant tropical climates, commonly grown in the southern regions of the Philippines, particularly in Davao and Mindanao.
Durian.uses: Eaten fresh for its rich, custard-like pungent flesh, or processed into commercial delicacies like durian candy, yema, and ice cream.

Durian___Healthy.symptoms: Oblong dark green leaves, large spiky durian fruits hanging from heavy branches.
Durian___Healthy.preventive_measures: Provide balanced organic compost and clean undergrowth.

Durian___Patch_Canker.symptoms: Reddish-brown liquid oozes from the trunk bark; bark turns soft, dark, and decays; leaves turn yellow and drop.
Durian___Patch_Canker.organic_treatment: Scrape off the cankered bark area. Paint the wound with a thick copper sulfate paste.
Durian___Patch_Canker.preventive_measures: Ensure excellent drainage. Do not injure the tree trunk during weeding.

watering_needs.frequency: High (Requires high annual rainfall and moist, shaded soil when young)
watering_needs.description: Requires consistent and abundant soil moisture. Highly susceptible to drying out; water daily and protect from extreme dry spells.

Mangosteen.description: Mangosteen (Garcinia mangostana), also called mangostan or purple mangosteen, is a tropical evergreen tree with edible fruit native to the Malay Peninsula and Borneo. It has been cultivated extensively in tropical Asia since ancient times. It is grown mainly in Southeast Asia, southwest India and other tropical areas such as Colombia, Puerto Rico and Florida, where the tree has been introduced. The tree grows from 6 to 25 m (19.7 to 82.0 ft) tall. The fruit of the mangosteen is sweet and tangy, juicy, somewhat fibrous, with fluid-filled vesicles and an inedible, deep reddish-purple colored rind (exocarp) when ripe. The fragrant edible flesh that surrounds each seed is the endocarp, i.e., the inner layer of the ovary. The seeds are of similar size and shape to almonds.
Mangosteen.habitat_ecology: Thrives in warm, humid, and rain-abundant tropical climates, commonly grown in the southern regions of the Philippines, particularly in Davao and Mindanao.
Mangosteen.uses: Eaten fresh by breaking the thick purple rind to reveal sweet, snowy-white segments, or boiled as a traditional herbal tea.

Mangosteen___Healthy.symptoms: Thick, leathery dark green leaves; dense conical crown; firm dark purple round mangosteens.
Mangosteen___Healthy.preventive_measures: Maintain high soil organic matter and mulch roots to preserve moisture.

Mangosteen___Gummosis.symptoms: Yellow latex/sap oozes from the branches, trunk, or fruit skin; sap stains fruits and makes flesh bitter.
Mangosteen___Gummosis.organic_treatment: Prune weak branches. Paint sap wounds with copper wash.
Mangosteen___Gummosis.preventive_measures: Protect trees from physical wind damage. Avoid overwatering.

watering_needs.frequency: Low to Moderate (Extremely drought-tolerant once established)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Sampalok.description: Tamarind (Tamarindus indica) is a leguminous tree bearing edible fruit that is indigenous to tropical Africa and naturalized in Asia. The genus Tamarindus is monotypic, meaning that it contains only this species. It belongs to the family Fabaceae. The tamarind tree produces brown, pod-like fruits that contain a sweet, tangy pulp, which is used in cuisines around the world. The pulp is also used in traditional medicine and as a dye. The wood can be used for woodworking, and tamarind seed oil can be extracted from the seeds. Its young leaves are used in Indian and Filipino cuisine. Because tamarind has multiple uses, it is cultivated worldwide in tropical and subtropical zones.
Sampalok.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Sampalok.uses: Unripe pods are the premier souring agent boiled for Sinigang broth; ripe pods are sweetened and rolled in sugar as a candy.

Sampalok___Healthy.symptoms: Feathery foliage, small leaves, long brown curved woody seed pods.
Sampalok___Healthy.preventive_measures: Requires almost no care once established; highly drought-resistant.

Sampalok___Sooty_Mold.symptoms: Black velvety layer of mold covers leaves and pods, reducing photosynthesis.
Sampalok___Sooty_Mold.organic_treatment: Spray soap solution to control aphids and scale insects. Wash mold off leaves.
Sampalok___Sooty_Mold.preventive_measures: Keep canopy open to the sun.

watering_needs.frequency: Low (Highly drought-tolerant; drops leaves and fruits in dry season)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Siniguelas.description: Spondias purpurea is a species of flowering plant in the cashew family, Anacardiaceae, that is native to tropical regions of the Americas, from Mexico to northern Colombia and the southwest Caribbean Islands. It has also been introduced to and naturalized to other parts of the American tropics, Southeast Asia, and West Africa. It is commonly known as jocote, which derives from the Nahuatl word xocotl, meaning any kind of sour or acidic fruit. Other common names include red mombin, Spanish plum, purple mombin, Jamaica plum, and hog plum. In countries where they are native, jocotes are also known more widely as jocote, ciruela, ciruelo, chiabal, cirgüela, or jobo (Mexico); ciruela calentana or ciruela huesito (Venezuela, Colombia); jocote tronador or sismoyo (Costa Rica); ciruela (Honduras); jocote comun (Nicaragua); ciruela or ciruela traqueadora (Panama); ciruela, ovo, ciriguela or jobito (Ecuador); and jocote, jobo, or pitarrillo (El Salvador). They were also introduced very early to the Philippines during the Spanish colonial era, where they are known as siniguelas, sineguelas, siriguelas, or sirhuelas (from Philippine Spanish ciruela or cirigüela).
Siniguelas.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Siniguelas.uses: Eaten whole (including the skin) when green and sour with rock salt, or when ripe, sweet, and juicy as a summer snack.

Siniguelas___Healthy.symptoms: Deciduous branches with small flowers, clusters of smooth green/red Spanish plums.
Siniguelas___Healthy.preventive_measures: Extremely hardy; thrives in dry soils.

Siniguelas___Fruit_Fly.symptoms: Fruits turn red prematurely, develop soft patches, and rot with small larvae inside.
Siniguelas___Fruit_Fly.organic_treatment: Hang pheromone traps around the canopy. Bury fallen fruit.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate (Water daily at base; keep foliage dry to avoid powdery mildew)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Melon.description: Cucumis melo, also known as melon or true melon, is a species of Cucumis that has been developed into many cultivated varieties. The fruit is a pepo. The flesh is either sweet or bland, with or without an aroma, and the rind can be smooth (such as honeydew), ribbed (such as European cantaloupe), wrinkled (such as Casaba melon), or netted (such as American cantaloupe). The species is sometimes referred to as muskmelon. However, there is no consensus about the usage of this term, as it can also be used as a specific name for the musky netted-rind American cantaloupe, or as a generic name for any sweet-flesh variety such the inodorous smooth-rind honeydew melon. The origin of melons is not known. Research has revealed that seeds and rootstocks were among the goods traded along the caravan routes of the Ancient World. Some botanists consider melons native to the Levant and Egypt, while others place their origin in Iran, India or Central Asia. Still others support an African origin; in modern times, wild melons can still be found in some African countries.
Melon.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Melon.uses: Shredded into thin ribbons to make the popular street drink Melon Juice, mixed with water, sugar, condensed milk, and ice.

Melon___Healthy.symptoms: Climbing fuzzy green vines, yellow flowers, round cantaloupes with netted skin.
Melon___Healthy.preventive_measures: Rest fruit on straw or plastic to avoid soil rot.

Melon___Powdery_Mildew.symptoms: White powdery patches cover leaves, leading to leaves drying up and sunburned fruits.
Melon___Powdery_Mildew.organic_treatment: Spray with dilute milk wash (1:9) or baking soda solution.
Melon___Powdery_Mildew.preventive_measures: Water at soil level early in the morning.

watering_needs.frequency: Moderate to High (Requires consistent watering; mulch with pine needles to prevent fruit rot)
watering_needs.description: Requires regular, even watering to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Allow the topsoil to dry slightly between watering cycles.

Strawberry.description: The garden strawberry (or simply strawberry; Fragaria × ananassa) is a widely grown hybrid plant cultivated worldwide for its fruit. The genus Fragaria, the strawberries, is in the rose family, Rosaceae. The fruit is appreciated for its aroma, bright red colour, juicy texture, and sweetness. It is eaten either fresh or in prepared foods such as jam, ice cream, and chocolates. Artificial strawberry flavourings and aromas are widely used in commercial products. Botanically, the strawberry is not a berry, but an aggregate accessory fruit. Each apparent 'seed' on the outside of the strawberry is actually an achene, a botanical fruit with a seed inside it. The garden strawberry was first bred in Brittany, France, in the 1750s via a cross of F. virginiana from eastern North America and F. chiloensis, which was brought from Chile by Amédée-François Frézier in 1714. Cultivars of F. × ananassa have replaced the woodland strawberry F. vesca in commercial production. In 2024, world production of strawberries exceeded ten million tons, led by China with 38% of the total.
Strawberry.habitat_ecology: Thrives in the cool, high-altitude climates of the Philippines, heavily cultivated in agricultural regions like Benguet (Baguio) and high-elevation zones of Mindanao.
Strawberry.uses: Eaten fresh with condensed milk, or processed into the famous Benguet strawberry jam, strawberry taho, and regional wines.

Strawberry___Healthy.symptoms: Trifoliate leaves, white flowers, bright red heart-shaped strawberries.
Strawberry___Healthy.preventive_measures: Mulch beds with pine needles (dulas) to keep berries off the wet soil.

Strawberry___Gray_Mold.condition_name: Gray Mold / Fruit Rot (Bulok sa Strawberry)
Strawberry___Gray_Mold.symptoms: Fuzzy gray mold covers developing or ripe strawberries, turning them into soft rot.
Strawberry___Gray_Mold.organic_treatment: Harvest ripe fruits daily. Prune old leaves. Spray copper fungicide or baking soda.
Strawberry___Gray_Mold.preventive_measures: Enforce strict mulching; avoid overhead watering.

watering_needs.frequency: Low to Moderate (Highly drought-resistant; thrives in dry soils)
watering_needs.description: Thrives in well-draining soil. Allow the soil to dry out significantly between watering cycles to avoid root rot.

Kasoy.description: Cashew is the common name of a tropical evergreen tree Anacardium occidentale, in the family Anacardiaceae. It is the source of the cashew nut and the cashew apple. The tree can grow as tall as 14 meters (46 feet). Cashews are native to tropical South America. Portuguese explorers encountered them in the 16th century and spread them to India and other tropical regions of the world. The dwarf cultivars, growing up to 6 m (20 ft), are the most profitable, maturing sooner and producing greater yields. In 2023, 3.9 million tons of cashew nuts were harvested globally, led by the Ivory Coast and India.
Kasoy.habitat_ecology: Widely grown in orchards, home backyards, and agricultural plantations across the lowlands of the Philippines.
Kasoy.uses: The roasted cashew nuts are a prized snack, while the yellow-red cashew apple is eaten fresh or squeezed into a tangy juice.

Kasoy___Healthy.symptoms: Leathery leaves, pear-shaped yellow-red cashew apples with kidney-shaped nuts below.
Kasoy___Healthy.preventive_measures: Prune lower branches to encourage a strong main canopy.

Kasoy___Anthracnose.symptoms: Reddish-brown spots on young leaves and flowers; developing cashew apples blacken and shrivel.
Kasoy___Anthracnose.organic_treatment: Prune affected flower twigs. Spray with copper sulfate solution.
